
### analysis building blocks 


* needed to track that <code>p</code> and <code>q</code> could point to same thing 
<hr class="vspace" />
* common prerequisite for all sorts of program analysis


### overly simple algorithm for points-to analysis 


* for each pointer/reference track which objects it can refer to
* if multiple paths: take union of all possible


### simple points-to analysis  {.smaller}

::::: {.columns}

:::: {.column width=30%}

```c
void example(int a) {
    int *p;
    int *q;
    q = malloc(...); // ID=1
    p = malloc(...); // ID=2
    // (A)
    if (a > 0) {
        p = q;
        // (B)
    }
    // (C)
    ...
}

```

::::

:::: {.column}

::: {.r-stack}

![](/static/texfig/simplePointsTo.figure-1.svg){.fragment .fade-in-then-out fragment-index=1 width=600px}

![](/static/texfig/simplePointsTo.figure-2.svg){.fragment .fade-in-then-out fragment-index=2 width=600px}

![](/static/texfig/simplePointsTo.figure-3.svg){.fragment .fade-in-then-out fragment-index=3 width=600px}

![](/static/texfig/simplePointsTo.figure-4.svg){.fragment .fade-in-then-out fragment-index=4 width=600px}

:::

::::

:::::

::: {.myoverbox .fragment .vlow fragment-index=2 .fade-in-then-out}

mark different versions of of p, q<br/>
track as if separate varaibles

:::


::: {.myoverbox .fragment .vlow fragment-index=3 .fade-in-then-out}

shown above: track each path separately <br/>
but means we'll have too many nodes in big program

:::

::: {.myoverbox .fragment .vlow fragment-index=4 .fade-in-then-out}

alternate idea: avoid path explosion by merging possible sets

:::
